---
title: "Decision Guide"
page-layout: full
---

::: {.hero}
::: {.container}
# Decision Guide

A practical guide for choosing the right DPmixGPD configuration. This is not a theory chapter; it is a "pick the next step" tool. When in doubt, start with a simple bulk model and add complexity only when diagnostics justify it.

[Go to Cookbook](cookbook.html){.btn .btn-primary}
[Open Kernels](kernels.html){.btn .btn-outline-light}
:::
:::

::: {.section}
::: {.container}

::: {.grid-3}

::: {.cardx}
### Step 1 - What is your outcome type?
Choose the modeling family based on the outcome's basic support.
- Positive and right-skewed (typical for costs, concentrations)
- Real-valued (can be negative)
- Time-to-event / survival (with censoring)
:::

::: {.cardx}
### Step 2 - Do you need a tail model?
Tail modeling is for rare extremes beyond a threshold.
Use a tail component if:
- the upper tail is scientifically important, and
- bulk-only fits understate extreme risk, or
- predictive checks show persistent underfit for high quantiles.
Otherwise, start bulk-only and revisit later.
:::

::: {.cardx}
### Step 3 - Do covariates or treatment effects matter?
- Unconditional: outcome-only modeling, simplest entry.
- Conditional: outcome depends on covariates.
- Causal: treatment vs control arms; treatment effects are derived quantities.
Use this decision early because it changes the workflow.
:::

:::

:::
:::

::: {.section}
::: {.container}

## A simple selection flow

::: {.grid-3}

::: {.cardx}
### A) I want a fast first fit
Start with an unconditional bulk model (no tail), run diagnostics, then expand.
- Next: [Cookbook](cookbook.html)
:::

::: {.cardx}
### B) I care about extremes
Start with a bulk model plus tail model. Treat threshold and tail uncertainty as part of the inferential target, not a post-hoc patch.
- Next: [Kernels](kernels.html)
- API details: [Kernel support table](pkgdown/reference/kernel_support_table.html)
:::

::: {.cardx}
### C) I need covariate effects or treatment effects
Use conditional/causal workflows from the start. Fit per-arm models (causal) or covariate-dependent models (conditional), then summarize effects.
- Next: [Roadmap](roadmap.html)
- Then: [Cookbook](cookbook.html)
:::

:::

:::
:::

::: {.section}
::: {.container}

## Practical heuristics (what usually works)

::: {.grid-3}

::: {.cardx}
### Bulk kernel choice
Pick the bulk kernel to match the "typical" range of the data:
- Strong right-skew and positive support: choose a positive-support kernel family.
- Symmetric real-valued outcomes: choose a real-valued kernel family.
If unsure, prioritize stability and interpretability first, then refine.
:::

::: {.cardx}
### Tail on/off decision
Turn on tail modeling when:
- high quantiles matter (e.g., 0.95-0.995),
- bulk-only predictive checks underfit extremes,
- or domain knowledge says extremes drive decisions.
If you are only doing central tendency inference, bulk-only is often enough.
:::

::: {.cardx}
### Backend choice (SB vs CRP)
Choose the backend based on your workflow preference:
- If you want a controlled truncation representation, stick-breaking is a natural fit.
- If you prefer a clustering metaphor and flexible component growth, CRP is intuitive.
When in doubt, start with the backend your existing workflow already supports most smoothly.
:::

:::

:::
:::

::: {.section}
::: {.container}

## Where to go for details
- Kernel availability and combinations: [Kernels](kernels.html)
- End-to-end workflows: [Cookbook](cookbook.html)
- Full API (functions, arguments, return objects): [Reference landing](reference.html) or [API index](pkgdown/reference/index.html)
- Design questions and future directions: [Discussions](https://github.com/arnabaich96/DPmixGPD/discussions)

:::
:::
